planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring before new growth emerges, or plant container divisions in spring to fall; white wood aster tolerates dry shade under established trees — one of its defining virtues — and requires no soil amendment in average woodland soils.

directions: Plant divisions in average to dry woodland soil in partial to full shade, including dry shade under established deciduous or conifer canopy — conditions under which most other shade perennials fail; white wood aster is the defining native aster for eastern North American shade gardens, producing wiry, zigzagging dark stems lined with heart-shaped leaves and topped in September and October with clouds of small white daisy flowers with yellow centers that age to wine-red, creating a bicolor effect as the season advances. It spreads steadily by rhizomes to form loose, natural-looking colonies that knit together a shaded woodland floor and naturalize beautifully at the base of large trees. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and control spread; this species is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and does not require supplemental irrigation even in dry-summer climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; bigleaf aster spreads robustly by rhizomes to form a dense groundcover colony — ideal for shaded slopes where erosion control and low-maintenance ground coverage are desired.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade, amended with leaf mold or compost to support the large, coarse foliage that distinguishes this species from all other native asters; bigleaf aster produces the largest basal leaves of any native aster — broad, rough-textured, and heart-shaped, often 4–6 inches across — which emerge as a dense, low rosette in spring and suppress weeds effectively before the 1–2 foot flowering stems rise in late summer, bearing pale lavender to white daisy flowers with yellow centers from August through September. It spreads vigorously by stout rhizomes to form extensive groundcover colonies on shaded slopes and woodland floors and is an excellent, low-maintenance native alternative to invasive groundcovers in areas of dense tree shade and moderate moisture. Divide every 3–4 years to rejuvenate colonies and share divisions; the bold foliage provides strong textural contrast alongside ferns, trilliums, and other fine-leaved woodland plants.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall for natural cold-moist stratification and spring germination, or stratify seed for 4–6 weeks at 40°F and sow in early spring; showy aster is native to coastal plain pine barrens and sandy, acidic, dry soils where it is most vigorous — avoid rich, moist garden soils that promote floppy growth.

directions: Sow seed in fall directly into well-drained, dry to average, acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; showy aster is a coastal plain native endemic to the pine barrens, sandy oak woodlands, and dry acidic uplands from New England to the Carolinas, where it produces vivid violet-purple ray flowers with yellow centers from August through October in conditions that would challenge most other flowering perennials. It is one of the most intensely colored native asters, the petals a rich violet-purple that stands out strongly against the sandy, sparse-vegetation landscapes it colonizes in the wild. Because it is adapted to lean, dry, infertile soils, planting in rich or moist garden conditions produces tall, floppy plants with diminished flowering — restrict soil richness and irrigation to keep plants compact and free-flowering. Divide or transplant with care, as the taproot resents disturbance once established.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in lean, well-drained, dry to average soil in full sun; aromatic aster is one of the most drought-tolerant and garden-worthy native asters and performs best in poor to average soils — rich, moist conditions promote floppy growth at the expense of the tightly mounded habit that makes this species so ornamentally distinguished.

directions: Plant in lean, dry to average, well-drained soil in full sun; aromatic aster is widely regarded as one of the finest native asters for garden use, forming a tightly mounded, self-supporting habit — rare among late-season asters, which often flop — covered from September into November with vivid blue-violet to lavender-purple flowers with yellow centers that provide some of the richest, most saturated color of the fall garden. The foliage is intensely aromatic when crushed, with a sharp, resinous scent reminiscent of cedar or pine that is distinctive and deer-deterring. It is outstanding in dry meadows, rock gardens, slopes, and parking-strip plantings where summer drought is routine, and provides critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs and native bees preparing for winter. Shear plants by half in late May or early June to keep the habit extra compact and encourage denser flowering; no staking is required in poor to average soils.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in average to moist, well-drained, woodland or open-edge soil in partial shade; Short's aster establishes readily and tolerates the dappled light of open woodland edges and north-facing slopes better than most asters, making it especially valuable for bridging the sun-to-shade transition zones in naturalistic garden designs.

directions: Plant divisions in average to moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun, where it performs best at open woodland edges, lightly shaded slopes, and north-facing exposures that receive morning sun and afternoon shade; Short's aster is a tall, graceful species native to the limestone woodlands and open forest edges of the central and eastern United States, producing branching stems 2–4 feet tall that bear abundant violet to blue daisy flowers with yellow centers from August through October, an important nectar source at a season when many woodland plants have finished blooming. Named for Dr. Charles Wilkins Short, a prominent 19th-century Kentucky botanist and physician, this species fills the niche of a shade-tolerant late-blooming aster that the more commonly grown garden asters cannot fill; it is an excellent choice for the understory garden in combination with golden rod, native ferns, and fall-blooming natives. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor; the plant self-seeds modestly and seedlings can be moved in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; golden groundsel spreads aggressively by stolons to form dense colonies that suppress weeds effectively — choose a site where this colonizing habit is an asset, such as a shaded bank or moist, wooded slope.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade, including deep shade under established trees where spring light penetrates before leaf-out — golden groundsel is one of the finest spring-blooming shade perennials native to eastern North America, producing a semi-evergreen rosette of attractive, heart-shaped basal leaves that remains green through winter and brightens in April and May with brilliant golden-yellow daisy flowers on 12–18 inch stems that stand out vividly against the dark tones of the shaded woodland floor. It spreads steadily and aggressively by surface stolons to form dense, weed-suppressing colonies that are highly effective as a native groundcover on shaded slopes, stream banks, and the moist understory of deciduous woodland; the low, persistent foliage rosettes hold the soil year-round. Note that Packera aurea (golden groundsel, golden ragwort) belongs to a distinct North American genus and should not be confused with the invasive Eurasian Senecio jacobaea (tansy ragwort), which is a different plant and a noxious weed in many states. Divide in spring to expand plantings or control spread.
